message:
perf(storage): single-IN+HAVING for multi-label AND, dedupe duplicate labels

`append_label_membership_filters` builds the SQL fragment that backs
`labels: Some(["a", "b", ...])` (the multi-label AND filter on
`list_issues`). The previous shape was N back-to-back EXISTS subqueries:

  AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM labels WHERE labels.issue_id = issues.id AND labels.label = ?)
  AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM labels WHERE labels.issue_id = issues.id AND labels.label = ?)
  ... × N

Each `EXISTS` re-enters the labels table with a correlated subquery.
On a backlog with thousands of issues × 5+ labels per filter the planner
ends up paying once per label per candidate row even when the labels
table is fully indexed. Single-label and zero-label cases were already
fast-pathed; only the >= 2 case had the N-EXISTS shape.

Two changes that compose into one perf win:

== Single subquery with IN + GROUP BY HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT) ==

Replace the N-EXISTS chain with a single subquery that asks the labels
table once and filters on the count of matching distinct labels per
issue:

  AND issues.id IN (
    SELECT issue_id FROM labels
    WHERE label IN (?, ?, ?, …, ?)
    GROUP BY issue_id
    HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT label) = ?
  )

The labels table is hit ONCE; the planner uses the (label) index to
narrow rows, GROUPS BY issue_id, and the HAVING clause does the
multi-label intersection in one pass. Same correctness as N-EXISTS
(an issue must have ALL N labels to satisfy `COUNT(DISTINCT label) =
N`), materially faster on the realistic case.

The placeholder list and the trailing count are constructed via
`std::fmt::Write` into the `String` accumulator (the trait was already
imported as `Write as _` at the top of the file). Parameters are
pushed in subquery order: the N label strings, then the count `i64`.
The count uses `i64::try_from(...).unwrap_or(i64::MAX)` defensive cast
so a pathologically long label list (>= 2^63 labels — never practical,
but free to handle) doesn't panic the binder.

== Dedupe before counting ==

A caller that passes `["frontend", "frontend"]` should not produce a
HAVING that requires `COUNT(DISTINCT label) = 2` — the duplicate
collapses to one logical label, so the intent is "must have
'frontend'". Pre-process the input through `HashSet<&str>` keyed on
`label.as_str()` so the unique set drives both the IN list and the
count. Order is preserved by walking the input vector and inserting
into the dedup set with `seen_labels_and.insert(...)` — items already
in the set don't append a second time.

Net effect: a duplicate label silently collapses to one (the
expected idempotent behavior), AND a deliberate three-label filter
where two are the same renders as a two-label HAVING that an issue
with both unique labels can actually satisfy.

The new test arm in `multi_label_filter_returns_intersection`
exercises `["core", "frontend", "frontend"]`, asserts the result
matches the deduped two-label intersection (one issue, `bd-l4`),
and pins the count via `count_issues_with_filters(...)` so any
future regression that drops the dedupe step (and falls into the
stricter HAVING = 3) shows up as a count mismatch — not just an
"empty result" failure that could be misread as a query bug.
